title: Lavender, herb profile
subtitle: Lavandula angustifolia: the western tradition's most versatile nervine
excerpt: A materia medica profile of lavender, its traditional actions, therapeutic uses across the nervous system, skin, and musculoskeletal systems, preparation methods, and safety. The most widely used aromatic herb in the European tradition, documented from Hildegard of Bingen to the EMA.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), lavender entry. Culpeper, The English Physician (1652). Hildegard of Bingen, Physica (12th century), earliest European documentation of medicinal lavender use. EMA HMPC monograph on Lavandulae flos (2011).

glossaryTerms.definition: The primary volatile constituent of lavender, responsible for its characteristic floral scent and its documented calming, anxiolytic, and analgesic effects on the nervous system.

content.text: Lavandula angustifolia, true lavender, English lavender, narrow-leaved lavender, is a woody aromatic perennial native to the western Mediterranean. It is the species used in both culinary and medicinal preparations; the spike lavender (L. latifolia) and lavandin hybrids (L. × intermedia) used in cheaper essential oils are not equivalent for internal therapeutic use. The dried flowers are the medicinal part. Its primary constituent is

(25, 45% of the essential oil), which acts on GABA-A receptors and serotonin pathways, the same systems targeted by benzodiazepines and certain antidepressants, but at much lower potency and without dependence risk.

Nervine (calming to the nervous system), anxiolytic (anti-anxiety), mild sedative, antispasmodic (smooth muscle and skeletal muscle), anti-inflammatory (topical), antiseptic (topical), rubefacient (warming, circulatory). Hildegard of Bingen (12th century) used lavender for 'migraine and a pure knowledge of God', the first recorded European therapeutic use. Culpeper (1652) lists it for 'the tremblings and passions of the heart'. Maud Grieve gives lavender as both a nervine and a wound-antiseptic herb.

Internal: mild anxiety, restlessness, sleep disturbance, mild low mood, tension headache (infusion). External: insect bites and stings (compress), muscle tension and soreness (bath, compress), mild skin irritation (compress or wash). The EMA's 2011 monograph confirms traditional use for mild anxiety, restlessness, and sleep disturbance via both oral and aromatic/inhalational routes.

Lavender has the strongest clinical evidence base of any nervine herb in the western tradition. A standardised lavender oil preparation (Silexan 80 mg daily) has been studied in multiple RCTs and found effective for generalised anxiety disorder and sleep disturbance at a concentration higher than a kitchen infusion. The infusion itself has traditional EMA status for mild anxiety and sleep disturbance; the mechanisms underlying the clinical trials are relevant to the infusion at lower potency.

Infusion: 3, 5 g dried flowers per 250 ml water, steeped covered for 10 minutes. One to three cups daily. Bath: 30 g in 500 ml concentrated infusion, added to warm bath. Compress: 5, 10 g in 200 ml water, cooled, applied to skin. Tincture: 1:5 in 40% spirit, 2, 4 ml daily (less commonly made at home; commercial preparations are more consistent).

Lavender at normal infusion and topical doses is safe for most adults, including in pregnancy at culinary and low therapeutic concentrations. No significant drug interactions are documented at infusion doses. Lavender essential oil is not the same as the flower infusion, the oil should not be ingested. For topical use on sensitive skin, test on a small area first. See your doctor for anxiety, low mood, or insomnia that is persistent or significantly impairing.
